stem: In MCP, who controls tools, resources, and prompts respectively?
options: All three are model-controlled
Tools are model-controlled (actions to invoke); resources are application-controlled (context to provide); prompts are user/application-controlled (reusable workflows)
Tools are user-controlled; resources are model-controlled; prompts are server-controlled
Control is negotiated per session between client and server
rationales: Only tools are invoked at the model's discretion.
The control model is the point: the model decides to act (tools), the application decides what context to supply (resources), users/applications invoke reusable templates (prompts).
Backwards on the first two.
The control assignments are part of the protocol design, not per-session negotiation.

stem: An MCP server for an internal API should expose the API's reference documentation and a way to place live orders. What is the right split?
options: Both as tools — everything an agent touches is a tool
Both as resources — servers should minimize tool count
Documentation as a prompt template; orders as a resource
Documentation as a resource (stable reference consulted before acting); order placement as a tool (dynamic action at moment of use)
rationales: Making reference material a tool forces a call just to learn what's true — the schema-fetch tax.
A resource can't place an order; replacing tools with resources prevents acting at all.
Prompts are reusable workflows, not documentation storage; and orders are the canonical tool case.
Resources for what is true and stable; tools for computation and change. Complements, not alternatives — good servers expose both.
sourceRef: ## 7 > What to Know (resource vs tool decision rule)

stem: Agents struggle to pick among many similar tools across connected servers. Someone proposes one natural-language entry tool that re-routes requests to the underlying tools. Why is this the wrong fix?
options: The aggregator hides the real tool surface from the model and tends to produce worse selection — improve descriptions and use progressive availability instead
Natural-language routing is prohibited by the MCP specification
Aggregators cannot pass parameters through to underlying tools
It works, but only for read-only tools
rationales: The model selects well when it can see accurate, distinct descriptions of a manageable surface. An opaque router replaces informed selection with a second, hidden selection problem.
Nothing prohibits it — it's just an anti-pattern.
Parameter passing is possible; the problem is the hidden decision, not plumbing.
Read-only vs mutating isn't the issue; visibility of the real surface is.

stem: When is building an MCP server clearly worth it over a custom in-app tool?
options: Whenever any external system is involved
When the integration must be fastest possible — MCP adds no overhead
When multiple AI clients or applications need the same integration — expose it once, reuse everywhere
When the workflow is deeply specific to a single application
rationales: External access alone doesn't require a protocol layer — a custom tool can call any API.
MCP's value is reuse and standardization, not raw speed.
Five AI tools needing the same ticketing data is the MCP sweet spot: one server, many clients.
That's the case for a custom tool inside the application — simpler than standing up a server.

stem: A team adopts MCP and assumes authentication, rate limiting, retries, and caching are now handled. What is the reality?
options: Correct — the protocol includes middleware for all four
MCP is a protocol, not a middleware platform — auth, rate limiting, retries, caching, authorization, and performance remain your system design responsibilities
The client library handles them if configured
Only authentication is included; the rest is yours
rationales: The protocol standardizes how capabilities are exposed, not how they're operated.
MCP solves the integration-shape problem. Everything operational still has to be designed and built — same as any service.
Client libraries implement the protocol; they don't run your infrastructure concerns.
Not even auth comes free — it must be designed into the server.

stem: An agent keeps using generic shell commands instead of a connected server's specialized dependency-analysis tool. What is the most likely fix?
options: Improve the MCP tool's description — when it beats generic alternatives, inputs/outputs, examples, key capabilities like transitive analysis
Remove the shell tool so the agent has no alternative
Rename the MCP tool to sort first alphabetically
Increase the server's connection priority
rationales: MCP tools compete with built-ins in one registry — the description is the tool's case for being chosen. "Analyzes code" loses to a familiar generic; a description explaining its edge wins.
The agent often legitimately needs generic tools too; removal breaks other work.
Alphabetical position isn't a selection signal.
No such priority mechanism drives model tool choice.

stem: A host auto-allows any MCP tool advertising `readOnlyHint: true`, skipping its confirmation flow. What is wrong with this policy?
options: Nothing — that is exactly what annotations are for
readOnlyHint doesn't exist; the standard hints are different
Read-only tools still consume rate limits, so confirmation is always required

Annotations are untrusted self-reports — a malicious or buggy server can label a destructive tool read-only; use annotations to pick which prompt to show, never to skip a security check policy requires
rationales: Annotations inform UI affordances; they are not a security boundary.
`readOnlyHint` is a standard hint, alongside destructive, idempotent, and open-world hints.
Rate limits aren't the issue — trust is.
Base real permission decisions on server trust level, user policy, tool identity, and actual operation risk. The server grading its own homework doesn't count.

stem: What is the practical rule for choosing between a JSON-RPC protocol error and a tool result with `isError: true` — and what goes wrong with each misuse?
options: Always prefer protocol errors; they're more visible to operators
Use whichever produces the shorter message

Failure before the tool's business logic could execute → protocol error; the tool reached its target and the operation failed → `isError: true`. Misusing `isError` for missing parameters makes the agent retry the same bad call; putting a 503 in a protocol error stops the agent from retrying later
Protocol errors for reads, isError for writes
rationales: Protocol errors don't reach the model as adaptable results — overusing them blinds the agent.
Message length is irrelevant to the semantics.
The boundary is where the failure occurred, and each misclassification causes a specific agent misbehavior — pointless identical retries, or abandoned retries that would have succeeded.
Read/write isn't the distinction; execution stage is.

stem: A host connects dozens of MCP servers. Which two mechanisms keep the tool surface manageable without a session restart when capabilities change?
options: Tool aliasing and schema compression
Tool search / progressive availability (pull tool definitions on demand per task), and `list_changed` notifications (servers announce changed tool sets so clients refresh)
Connection pooling and lazy handshakes
A master server that proxies all the others
rationales: Neither is an MCP surface-management mechanism.

Progressive availability spends context only on tools about to be used; `list_changed` lets a new capability appear mid-session. Design consequence: write descriptions that read well in isolation, since tools may be discovered via search.
Transport-level concerns; they don't shrink the model's tool surface.
That's the aggregator anti-pattern wearing a trench coat.

stem: In Claude Code, where should (a) a documentation server the whole team needs and (b) a server needing your personal API credentials be configured?
options: Both in `.mcp.json` so configuration stays in one place
Both in user scope for maximum availability
(a) user scope; (b) project scope
(a) project scope — `.mcp.json` at the repo root, checked in; (b) local or user scope inside `~/.claude.json`, where credentials stay on your machine
rationales: `.mcp.json` is checked into version control — personal credentials in it leak to everyone who clones.
User scope hides the team server from teammates, who each must configure it manually.
Backwards: the shared server belongs in the shared file; the credentialed one must stay private.
Scope = visibility: project scope is shared via the repo; local and user scopes live in `~/.claude.json` and never leave the developer's machine.

stem: The same MCP server name exists at local, project, and user scope with different configurations. What does Claude Code do?
options: Connects once using the local definition — precedence is local > project > user, and the entire winning entry is used with no field merging
Connects three times, once per scope
Merges the three configurations field by field
Refuses to start until the collision is resolved
rationales: The precedence is deliberate: your local definition can point at a staging endpoint without touching the team's `.mcp.json`. One connection, whole-entry wins.
Name collisions resolve to a single connection, not three.
Fields are never merged across scopes — partial-override setups silently don't work.
Collisions are resolved by precedence, not treated as errors.

stem: How do MCP prompts appear to users in Claude Code?
options: As automatic behaviors the model applies silently
As entries in the settings menu
As slash commands, typically named `mcp__<server>__<prompt>` to disambiguate across servers
They are not accessible in Claude Code
rationales: Prompts are user/application-invoked, not silent model behavior.
They surface in the command interface, not settings.
The naming pattern tells you which server a prompt came from when several are connected.
They are fully supported — as slash commands.
sourceRef: ## 7 > MCP in Claude Code (prompts as slash commands)
